summary:
The Russo-Turkish War of 1877–78 was a conflict between the Russian Empire and the [[event:94|Ottoman Empire]] that resulted in a decisive Russian victory, substantial Ottoman territorial losses, and the creation of several new Balkan states — a reshaping of southeastern Europe whose consequences extended into the twentieth century. The war was triggered by Ottoman suppression of the Bulgarian uprising of 1876, the atrocities of which (the 'Bulgarian Horrors') generated intense European outrage and provided Russia with both popular justification and diplomatic cover for military action.

Russian forces crossed the Danube in June 1877 on two fronts: a main force in Bulgaria and a Caucasian force advancing through eastern Anatolia. The Bulgarian campaign was more difficult than expected: the Ottoman fortress of Plevna (Pleven) held out for five months before falling in December 1877 after a costly siege, costing the Russians enormous casualties. Once Plevna fell, Russian forces advanced rapidly through the Balkans and reached the outskirts of Constantinople (Istanbul) by January 1878, when an armistice was concluded.

The Treaty of San Stefano (March 1878) created a large Bulgarian state that would have been a Russian client dominating the Balkans — a prospect alarming to Britain and Austria-Hungary. The subsequent Congress of Berlin, convened by Bismarck, revised San Stefano drastically: Bulgaria was reduced and divided, Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro gained formal independence, and Austria-Hungary received Bosnia-Herzegovina as a protectorate. The gap between Russian military sacrifice and diplomatic outcome generated lasting resentment in St. Petersburg, while the Eastern Question remained unresolved and a source of great-power competition until the First World War.
